1: “The land enjoyed ten years of rest (šāqaṭ) during his reign.” The verb šāqaṭ (be quiet/undisturbed/at rest) is the term used for the covenant rest of the land (cf. Josh 11:23; 14:15; Judg 3:11). It is distinct from the political-strategic peace and carries connotations of YHWH’s active protection — the land rests because God stands guard. MKT renders it “rest” rather than “peace” to maintain this theological resonance.
2: The formulaic evaluation: “Asa did what was good and right (haṭṭôḇ wĕhayyāšār) in the sight of the LORD his God.” This double-virtue formula — ṭôḇ (good) and yāšār (right/straight/upright) — is the standard Chronicler commendation (cf. 2 Chr 31:20: Hezekiah; Deut 12:25, 28 for the underlying standard). Both terms have covenantal weight: yāšār is the conduct that “fits the straight path,” the path YHWH himself walks.
3: The reform items: foreign altars (mizbĕḥôt hannēkār), high places (bāmôt), sacred pillars (maṣṣēḇôt), Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm). This is the standard Deuteronomic list of forbidden cult objects (Deut 7:5; 12:3). The Chronicler uses the full Deuteronomic vocabulary of cult purge without abbreviation — he is presenting Asa as the archetype of Deuteronomic kingship, the king who implements Moses’s commands.
4: Asa “commanded” (wayyōʾmer — literally “said to,” used in the sense of a royal command) the people to seek (lidrōš) YHWH and to observe (laʿăśôt) the law and commandments. The verb dāraš appears here for the first time in Asa’s narrative as the defining action of his reign. The Chronicler structures Asa’s narrative as a sustained meditation on what it means to “seek” or “not seek” YHWH (dāraš appears in 14:4, 7; 15:2, 4, 12, 13, 15; 16:12 — the whole arc of Asa’s reign).
5: Asa removes high places and incense altars from every town in Judah — the country-wide scope distinguishes his reform from Abijah’s, which was primarily about legitimate cult maintenance. The phrase “incense altars” (ḥammānîm) occurs in Lev 26:30; Isa 17:8; 27:9; Ezek 6:4, 6 — always in condemnation. The result: “the kingdom was at peace under him” — šāqaṭ again, connecting reform and rest as cause and effect.
6: “He built fortified cities because the LORD gave him rest (nûaḥ lô)” — a different word for rest here: nāḥaḥ (give rest to) rather than šāqaṭ. The Chronicler uses two related but distinct rest-terms: šāqaṭ for the state of rest the land enjoys; nûaḥ for the rest that YHWH actively gives. The building program during the rest period is characteristic of the Chronicler’s understanding that covenant faithfulness creates the conditions for constructive enterprise (cf. Solomon’s building during rest, 2 Chr 8:3-6).
7: Asa’s speech to Judah contains the confession “The land is still ours because we have sought (dāraštānû) the LORD our God. We sought him and he has given us rest on every side.” The causal logic — seeking God → divine gift of rest — is the theological axiom of Chronicles. The land belongs to those who seek its true owner; rest is not achieved militarily but received as a covenant gift. The doubled dāraš in this verse emphasizes the point.
8: The army muster: 300,000 from Judah (large shield and spear) plus 280,000 from Benjamin (bow and small shield) = 580,000 total. The tribal distribution — Judah with the heavy-infantry weapons, Benjamin with archery — reflects the traditional military specializations in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Judg 20:16 for Benjaminite archery; 2 Chr 17:17 for similar numbers in Jehoshaphat’s reign).
9: Zerah the Cushite with a million men and 300 chariots. “Zerah the Cushite” is not mentioned in Kings and may be Osorkon I of Egypt (a Libyan pharaoh, c. 924-889 BCE, since “Cush” sometimes refers to Libya/Nubia in this period) or a separate Cushite commander acting independently. The “million” (ʾeleph could mean either “a thousand units” or literally one million) continues the hyperbolic military register. The key point is the vast disproportion — Judah’s 580,000 face a force described as overwhelming.
10: The battle takes place in the Valley of Zephathah near Mareshah (in the Shephelah, southwest Judah). Mareshah was a significant border city (cf. Mic 1:15) near the main route from the coastal plain into the hill country. The geographical setting is historically plausible for a Cushite/Egyptian incursion from the south and west.
11: Asa’s prayer is a model of the genre: “LORD, there is no one like you to help the powerless (ʾên ʿimmĕkā liʿzôr bên rab lĕʾên kōaḥ — literally ‘there is nothing with you to help between the many and the one without strength’) against the mighty.” The logic: YHWH is distinguished precisely by his ability to give victory to the weak; the disproportion of forces is not an impediment but a demonstration-opportunity. “We rely on you (šāʿannû ʿāleykā) and have come against this vast army in your name” — the verb šāʿan (rely/lean on) that will mark the whole Asa narrative.
12: “The LORD struck down (wayyiggōp̄) the Cushites.” The verb nāgap̄ (strike/plague) is the term used for YHWH’s decisive military action — the same verb used for the death of Jeroboam (13:20) and the Exodus plagues. The Cushites “fled” (wayyānūsû) — flight before YHWH’s army is the standard description of divine-warrior victory (cf. Exod 14:27; Josh 10:11).
13: The pursuit extends all the way to Gerar — deep into Philistine territory, south of Gaza. The description “none could survive, for they were shattered before the LORD and his army” uses the phrase “shattered” (nišbĕrû) and the divine passive “before the LORD.” The plunder is described as “enormous” (rabbâ mĕʾōd) — very great, continuing the scale-language of the whole campaign.
14: The surrounding towns are struck with the “terror of the LORD” (paḥad YHWH) — a theological term for the overwhelming fear that precedes YHWH’s advance (cf. Exod 15:16; Deut 2:25; 11:25). This is not merely the psychological effect of military defeat but a specific divine disposition toward enemies of his covenant people. The towns fall not because of Judah’s military force but because of YHWH’s advance-terror.
15: The pastoralists’ camps (ōhĕlê miqneh — “tents of livestock”) are raided and large numbers of sheep and camels taken. The return to Jerusalem concludes the campaign. The Chronicler carefully notes that the army returns to Jerusalem — the center of the covenant community’s life — not to Asa’s palace. The victory is a covenant victory that properly ends in the covenant city.
